name: Sum Array
description: Given an array of numbers, return the sum of all elements. The function should handle the empty array (return 0). The signature is `def sum_array(arr): ...` (or equivalent).
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (60%): Algorithm correctness for populated and empty arrays.
   - 60/60 — The code, read as pseudocode, computes the sum of all elements for both populated and empty arrays. No wrong-answer logic visible in the loop / built-in / reduction / recursion.
   - 45/60 — Demonstrates the right idea but misses one edge case visible in the code (e.g. doesn't return 0 for empty input, but you can point to the line where this happens).
   - 25/60 — Wrong algorithm or wrong answer on the typical input shape, with evidence in the code (e.g. multiplies instead of adds, never accumulates, returns the wrong variable).
   - 0/60 — Code is structurally unrunnable as a sum (e.g. no loop at all and no built-in invoked, or returns a constant).
2. Code Quality (25%): Naming, style, and idiomatic Python.
   - 25/25 — Descriptive names, single clean expression of the chosen approach, no shadowing of built-ins, parses cleanly.
   - 18/25 — Works but has at most one stylistic blemish you can name (an unnecessary intermediate variable, a shadowed built-in like `sum`, or a localized parse error).
   - 12/25 — Multiple stylistic blemishes you can point to in the code, or unidiomatic structure that obscures the algorithm.
   - 5/25 — Hard to read end-to-end; you cannot describe the approach without re-reading.
3. Efficiency (15%): Single-pass O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary space.
   - 15/15 — Single-pass O(n) accumulation, no extra allocations or per-iteration overhead visible.
   - 10/15 — O(n) but with extra work you can cite (e.g. computes `len(arr)` inside a per-element loop, builds an intermediate list, calls `sum` on a generator that itself loops).
   - 5/15 — Worse than O(n) — quadratic, repeated traversals, or recursion that re-walks settled prefixes.
rubric_unstructured: Score the submission on correctness (60%), code quality (25%), and efficiency (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the function should return the sum of all elements for both populated and empty arrays without IndexError, RecursionError, or wrong-answer logic; a localized syntax error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The code should be Pythonic — descriptive names, no shadowing of built-ins like `sum`, and clean implementation of the chosen approach (loop, built-in, reduce, recursion); parse-error hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized. The approach should be a reasonable single-pass O(n) accumulation without unnecessary work or duplication.

name: Palindrome Check
description: Implement `boolean isPalindrome(String s)` that returns `true` if the input reads the same forwards and backwards, ignoring case and non-alphanumeric characters, and `false` otherwise. The empty string is a palindrome. Examples: `"A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"` → `true`; `"race a car"` → `false`; `""` → `true`.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (60%): Normalises input and compares correctly for all cases.
   - 60/60 — Read as pseudocode, the code normalises (lower-case + strip non-alphanumeric) and compares the normalised string to its reverse. Mentally trace one mixed-case input and one with punctuation and the result matches expectation.
   - 45/60 — Right approach but you can point to one edge-case gap in the code (e.g. empty-string path returns `false`, or odd-length is off by one, or only letters are normalised so digits get stripped).
   - 25/60 — Wrong on the typical input shape with code evidence — e.g. compares before normalising, has a silent always-true expression like `s.equals(s)`, or uses `==` to compare strings.
   - 0/60 — Structurally cannot return the right answer (e.g. always returns a constant, body is empty).
2. Code Quality (25%): Idiomatic Java style and readability.
   - 25/25 — Idiomatic Java, descriptive names, the chosen approach (two-pointer, reverse-and-compare, StringBuilder, Stream) is implemented in a single clean expression.
   - 18/25 — Works but has one blemish you can cite — a localized compile error, an unnecessary intermediate buffer, or a Stream chain that's harder to read than the equivalent loop.
   - 12/25 — Multiple visible style issues — uses `==` instead of `.equals`, mixes recursion with imperative state, or names are non-descriptive (`x`, `tmp`).
   - 5/25 — Hard to follow; you have to re-read the body to be sure what it does.
3. Efficiency (15%): Linear time and reasonable space for the chosen approach.
   - 15/15 — Linear time and reasonable space for the chosen approach.
   - 10/15 — Linear but with extra allocations you can cite (e.g. builds three intermediate strings when one would do).
   - 5/15 — Quadratic substring recursion, stack-blowing on long inputs, or repeated full-string traversals per character.
rubric_unstructured: Score the `isPalindrome` method on correctness (60%), code quality (25%), and efficiency (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — it should correctly return `true` exactly when the input reads the same forward and backward after lower-casing and stripping non-alphanumeric characters, including the empty string and inputs with mixed case or punctuation, with no off-by-one errors on odd-length strings and no silent always-true logic; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The Java should be idiomatic — uses `.equals` rather than `==`, applies a clean approach (two pointers, reverse-and-compare, StringBuilder, Streams, or recursion), and reads cleanly; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized. The implementation should not be quadratic or stack-overflow-prone, and should not allocate more strings than the chosen approach requires.

name: LRU Cache
description: Design an in-memory Least Recently Used (LRU) cache as a class `LRUCache<K, V>` with constructor `(capacity: number)`, `get(key: K): V | undefined`, `put(key: K, value: V): void` (overwriting if present), and `size(): number`. Both `get` and `put` count as 'use'. When `size > capacity` after a `put`, evict the least-recently-used entry. Both operations should be O(1) amortized.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (40%): All four operations implement the spec faithfully.
   - 40/40 — All four operations match the spec when traced — `get` returns `undefined` for misses and refreshes recency; `put` overwrites and refreshes; eviction removes the LRU entry when `size > capacity`. You can point to the line that updates recency on `get`.
   - 30/40 — Right structure but one cited edge case is wrong — e.g. returns `null` instead of `undefined`, or doesn't refresh recency on `get` (you can name the line).
   - 18/40 — Eviction logic visibly evicts the wrong entry, or `put` of an existing key inserts a duplicate, or `capacity = 0` would loop.
   - 5/40 — `get` / `put` do not implement the spec at all (no eviction code, or returns the wrong thing for every call).
2. Data Structures (25%): Correct data structure choice for O(1) recency tracking.
   - 25/25 — `Map` (insertion-ordered) or hash + doubly-linked list; recency state lives in one place, eviction reads it directly.
   - 18/25 — Right idea but with redundancy you can cite (e.g. parallel `Map` + array for ordering that can drift out of sync).
   - 10/25 — Uses a plain object as ordered map, or a single array scanned with `indexOf`, or two structures that don't actually express recency.
   - 5/25 — Wrong tool entirely — no order tracking, or relies on insertion order of `{}` (not guaranteed in older JS).
3. Efficiency (20%): get and put are O(1) amortized.
   - 20/20 — `get` and `put` are O(1) amortized — you can point to the constant-time recency update.
   - 14/20 — One operation is super-constant — e.g. `O(log n)` via a sorted structure, or O(n) only on eviction.
   - 8/20 — `O(n)` scan on every `get` or `put` (e.g. `indexOf` to find LRU), or re-allocates the storage on every write.
   - 3/20 — Worse — repeated full traversals per op.
4. Code Quality (15%): Generic types, descriptive names, and a clean single approach.
   - 15/15 — Generic `<K, V>`, descriptive names, a single clean approach, no dead fields.
   - 11/15 — One blemish you can cite — `any` in one place, or a redundant private field, or a localized compile error.
   - 7/15 — Mixed approaches (parts written for a different data structure), unidiomatic TS, or non-descriptive names throughout.
   - 3/15 — Hard to follow; the API does not match the spec signatures.
rubric_unstructured: Score the LRUCache on correctness (40%), data-structure choice (25%), efficiency (20%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — it should implement the spec faithfully (`get` returns the value or `undefined`, `put` overwrites and refreshes recency, both `get` and `put` count as use, the LEAST-recently-used entry is evicted once `size > capacity`, `undefined` (not `null`) is returned for misses, `capacity = 0` does not blow up, and the most-recently-used entry is never mis-evicted); a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The data structure should be one that can deliver the recency semantics — `Map` (insertion-ordered in TypeScript), or hash + doubly-linked list, or equivalent — without duplicate recency entries and without abusing plain object as an ordered map. Both `get` and `put` should be O(1) amortized; no O(n) scans like `indexOf` to find the LRU, and no accidental re-allocation on every `put`. Stylistically, use generics `<K, V>` rather than `any`, and implement the chosen approach idiomatically without redundant fields or awkward APIs; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Dijkstra Shortest Path
description: Implement Dijkstra's single-source shortest-path algorithm with signature `std::vector<long long> dijkstra(int n, const std::vector<std::vector<std::pair<int, int>>>& graph, int source)`. `n` is the number of nodes (0..n-1); `graph[u]` is a list of `{v, w}` outgoing edges with non-negative weights. Return shortest distances from `source` to every node, with `LLONG_MAX` for unreachable nodes. Edge weights fit in `int`; distances accumulate in `long long`.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (45%): Recognisable Dijkstra producing correct shortest-path distances.
   - 45/45 — Recognisable Dijkstra: initialises `dist[source] = 0`, repeatedly extracts the closest unsettled node, relaxes its edges. Traced on a small graph the output matches shortest-path expectation. Unreachable nodes keep the sentinel.
   - 32/45 — Dijkstra's structure but one cited bug — e.g. doesn't initialise `dist[source]`, returns wrong sentinel, or reads `graph[v]` instead of `graph[u]` in the relaxation.
   - 18/45 — Wrong algorithm family visible in the code — BFS treats edge weights as 1, DFS explores without distance ordering, Bellman-Ford-style |V|·|E| relaxation.
   - 0/45 — Cannot be traced to a shortest-path algorithm at all (e.g. body is empty, returns a copy of the input).
2. Efficiency (25%): O((V+E) log V) with heap and settled-node skipping.
   - 25/25 — O((V+E) log V) — uses a heap / set, pops the min, and avoids re-processing visibly settled nodes.
   - 18/25 — O((V+E) log V) but with one cited inefficiency — re-walks the whole graph per relaxation, or doesn't skip stale heap entries.
   - 10/25 — O(V²) linear-scan Dijkstra (no heap), but still correct.
   - 5/25 — Worse than O(V²) — repeatedly re-runs the inner loop, or scans the full edge list per vertex on every iteration.
3. Data Structures (15%): Heap or set frontier with correct adjacency traversal.
   - 15/15 — `std::priority_queue` (min-heap) or `std::set` as the frontier; adjacency list consumed as edges (no transposed indices).
   - 10/15 — Unsorted vector / array used as a heap-equivalent with explicit extraction-of-min — works but bypasses the standard tool.
   - 5/15 — Linear scan over all vertices per step.
   - 0/15 — Wrong adjacency representation (e.g. iterates `graph[v]` for each `u`) or no frontier structure at all.
4. Code Quality (15%): Readable C++ with meaningful names and linear control flow.
   - 15/15 — Readable C++: distance array, frontier, and edge endpoints have meaningful names; control flow is linear.
   - 11/15 — One blemish you can cite — `using namespace std` is fine, but mixing `.first/.second` with structured bindings inconsistently, or a localized compile error.
   - 7/15 — Multiple visible style issues — single-letter names everywhere, dead branches, redundant work in the inner loop.
   - 3/15 — Hard to read end-to-end; you cannot quickly tell which variable is the distance and which is the frontier.
rubric_unstructured: Score the Dijkstra implementation on correctness (45%), efficiency (25%), data-structure choice (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the code should actually run Dijkstra (not BFS, DFS, or Bellman-Ford), return shortest distances for every node with `LLONG_MAX` for unreachable ones, initialize `dist[source] = 0`, not crash on out-of-range source, and fail clearly or document negative-weight handling; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. Performance-wise it should skip stale heap entries (O((V+E) log V)), use `long long` for accumulation to avoid overflow, not re-process settled nodes, and not iterate the whole graph per relaxation. The frontier should be a `std::priority_queue` or `std::set` rather than a linear scan, and the adjacency list should be consumed correctly without transpose mistakes. Code quality is about readability: meaningful names for the distance array, frontier, and edge endpoints, clear control flow, and a consistent sentinel choice that doesn't overflow when added to a distance. `LLONG_MAX` is fine, `std::numeric_limits<long long>::max()` is fine, and stylistic preferences like `using namespace std` or `.first`/`.second` vs structured bindings are not penalized; compile hygiene also lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Binary Search Tree
description: Implement a Binary Search Tree for `Integer` keys with the following API: `void insert(int key)` (idempotent on duplicates), `boolean contains(int key)`, `boolean delete(int key)` returning `true` iff the key existed, `List<Integer> inorder()` returning keys in ascending order, and `int size()`. `delete` must correctly handle the leaf, single-child, and two-children (replace with in-order successor or predecessor and remove that successor) cases.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (40%): All five BST methods correct, including two-children delete.
   - 40/40 — All five methods (`insert`, `contains`, `delete`, `inorder`, `size`) traced on a small tree behave per spec. `delete` with two children visibly replaces with successor (or predecessor) and then removes that node from its old position.
   - 28/40 — Insert / contains / inorder / size correct, but `delete` has one cited bug — e.g. forgets to remove the successor after copying it, or mishandles the single-child case.
   - 15/40 — Two-children delete is broken or absent; or `inorder` does not return keys in ascending order on the traced example.
   - 5/40 — Two or more methods are wrong or missing per the spec signature.
2. Data Structures (30%): Genuine BST with encapsulated Node and BST ordering invariant.
   - 30/30 — Genuine BST: a `Node` class with `key`, `left`, `right`; the BST invariant is honoured by insert / search. The chosen style (recursive or iterative) is applied consistently.
   - 21/30 — BST but with leaked encapsulation you can cite — public `Node` fields, exposed root reference, or duplicate keys allowed when spec said idempotent.
   - 12/30 — Mostly a tree but the BST ordering invariant is not visibly enforced (e.g. `insert` puts everything on one side).
   - 0/30 — Delegates to `java.util.TreeSet` / `TreeMap` instead of building a BST.
3. Efficiency (15%): O(1) size and O(n) inorder without quadratic concatenation.
   - 15/15 — `size()` is O(1) (a maintained field), and `inorder` builds the result list in one pass without quadratic concatenation.
   - 11/15 — `size()` walks the tree on every call (O(n)) but other operations are sound.
   - 7/15 — Multiple O(n)-per-call costs you can cite — e.g. `size()` walks tree AND `inorder` rebuilds via string concatenation.
   - 3/15 — Recursion blows the stack on small inputs (no tail call elimination in Java, and the recursion depth is unbounded against the input size with no acknowledgement).
4. Code Quality (15%): Idiomatic Java with descriptive names and consistent style.
   - 15/15 — Idiomatic Java: `List<Integer>` return types, descriptive method names, consistent recursion or iteration.
   - 11/15 — One blemish you can cite — uses `ArrayList<Integer>` directly in the return type, or a localized compile error, or one method written in a different style than the rest.
   - 7/15 — Mixed styles, non-descriptive names (`n1`, `tmp`), dead branches.
   - 3/15 — Hard to follow; methods do not match the public spec signatures.
rubric_unstructured: Score the BST on correctness (40%), data-structure design (30%), efficiency (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — all five methods should behave per spec (`insert` is idempotent on duplicates; `delete` correctly handles the leaf, single-child, and two-children cases including removing the successor afterwards; `inorder` returns keys ascending; `contains` and `size` return correct values; no NullPointerException on missing keys); a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The data structure should be a genuine BST with an encapsulated private `Node` inner class (private fields), the BST invariant should be honoured, and the implementation must not delegate to `java.util.TreeSet` / `TreeMap`. Performance-wise, `size()` should be O(1) cached rather than recomputed by tree-walk, `inorder` should not rely on string concatenation or repeatedly resize fixed-size arrays, and degenerate inputs should not blow the stack (or the limitation should be acknowledged). Stylistically, the Java should be idiomatic — `List<Integer>` in return types, clear method names, and consistent recursion or iteration style; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Concurrent Worker Pool
description: Implement `func ParallelMap[T, R any](inputs []T, workers int, fn func(T) R) []R` that processes the inputs concurrently using `workers` goroutines while preserving input order — `out[i]` must be the result of applying `fn` to `inputs[i]`. Treat `workers <= 0` as `workers = 1`. When `workers > len(inputs)`, only `len(inputs)` goroutines should be spawned. The function must not deadlock, leak goroutines, or panic on empty input. `fn` is safe to call concurrently.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (35%): Preserves input order, normalises workers≤0, handles empty input.
   - 35/35 — Traced on `inputs = [a,b,c]` with any worker count, `out[i] == fn(inputs[i])` for every i. `workers <= 0` is visibly normalised to 1 (you can point to the guard). Empty input returns an empty slice.
   - 25/35 — One spec gap you can cite — e.g. `workers <= 0` not normalised, or output order is by completion time rather than input index.
   - 15/35 — Output indices visibly don't correspond to input indices, or the function panics on the empty-input path you can read.
   - 0/35 — Cannot be traced to a parallel map over `inputs` (e.g. no goroutines, no channel, returns nil).
2. Concurrency Safety (35%): No data races, goroutine leaks, deadlocks, or loop-capture bugs.
   - 35/35 — Every spawned goroutine is waited for (you can point to the `wg.Add` / `wg.Done` pairing or the channel-drain that serves the same purpose). No writes to the same index from multiple goroutines. No channels that can deadlock for the traced inputs.
   - 25/35 — Synchronisation works in the happy path but you can point to one path where a goroutine might not finish — e.g. on the `workers > len(inputs)` path some workers receive zero jobs but the channel is closed correctly.
   - 15/35 — A specific safety bug visible in the code — loop-variable capture by reference, writes to a shared map without locking, or a channel that's sent on without a receiver on a code path you can trace.
   - 0/35 — Code can clearly deadlock on any input (e.g. sends N items into a 0-capacity channel before any goroutine starts) or races on the result slice (multiple goroutines `append`-ing to the same slice).
3. Efficiency (15%): Actually parallelises without mutex serialisation on result writes.
   - 15/15 — Actually parallelises — `min(workers, len(inputs))` goroutines, each consuming jobs, with no mutex serialising the result writes (indexed writes are race-free).
   - 11/15 — Parallel but with one cited bottleneck — a single mutex around the result write, or unnecessarily-small channel buffer that backs up the producer.
   - 7/15 — Mostly serial — one goroutine doing all the work, or a global mutex around `fn`.
   - 3/15 — Spawns goroutines but the structure makes them run one at a time.
4. Code Quality (15%): Idiomatic Go with channels and WaitGroup used cleanly.
   - 15/15 — Idiomatic Go: channels and `sync.WaitGroup` used cleanly, no unnecessary imports (`errgroup` when there are no errors), no superfluous mutexes.
   - 11/15 — One blemish you can cite — a redundant mutex, a deferred close where a direct close would do, or a localized compile error.
   - 7/15 — Mixed paradigms (channels AND shared-state mutex doing the same job), single-letter names throughout, or unidiomatic for-select patterns.
   - 3/15 — Hard to follow; you cannot tell at a glance which side of the channel is the producer.
rubric_unstructured: Score `ParallelMap` on correctness (35%), concurrency safety (35%), efficiency (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the output slice must preserve input order (`out[i]` corresponds to `inputs[i]`); `workers <= 0` must be treated as `workers = 1`; at most `len(inputs)` goroutines should be spawned; and the code must not panic on empty input. A localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. Concurrency safety is critical — no data races (`go test -race` clean), no goroutine leaks when `workers > len(inputs)` or on empty input, no deadlock on capacity-zero channels, no loop-variable-capture bug, and every spawned goroutine must be waited for. The implementation should actually parallelise — no serialisation behind a mutex when indexed writes are already race-free, and channel / slice capacities should be sized to avoid unnecessary blocking, with goroutine count bounded by `min(workers, len(inputs))`. The Go style should be idiomatic — channels and goroutines used cleanly, `sync.WaitGroup` for synchronization, no superfluous mutexes, and no unnecessary external dependencies; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.
